one_line_takeaway: A narrative survey arguing that CAV data-sharing delivers real consumer benefits but is regulated only by generic (non-CAV-specific) privacy laws worldwide, leaving a governance gap the authors call a 'privacy vacuum.'
key_finding: The paper's central conclusion (Section VI.B and the Conclusion) is that although general-purpose data-protection regimes exist worldwide (GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, PIPEDA/CPPA, PDPA, DSL/PIPL, Privacy Act 1988, PDPPL), no country has enacted a legal framework specifically tailored to CAV data sharing; this regulatory gap is characterized as a 'privacy vacuum' that automakers and third parties are described as exploiting to collect data without adequately informed consent and to retain it longer than necessary. The paper argues this creates tension between the consumer benefits of pervasive CAV data sharing (Section IV) and the privacy/security risks it enables (Section V), and calls for domain-specific CAV privacy regulation covering consent, data minimization, retention/deletion, and enforcement penalties.
core_contribution: A narrative (non-systematic) literature review of data sharing in connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs). The paper's own contributions, as stated by the authors, are: (1) an overview of real-world CAV data-sharing mechanisms tracing the journey of data from sensors to external entities via V2V/V2I/V2P/V2N/V2C/V2D channels; (2) a catalog of ten consumer benefit use cases enabled by data sharing (e.g., emergency assistance, personalized driving recommendations, personalized maintenance alerts, real-time traffic/parking info, adaptive climate control, automatic roadside assistance, smart-home integration, energy optimization, tailored infotainment, scheduled service reminders); (3) an enumeration of nine privacy-risk categories arising from CAV data sharing (advertising exploitation, insurance premium manipulation, targeted marketing, vehicle hacking/unauthorized access, unauthorized disclosure of travel patterns, financial manipulation by service providers, stalking/harassment risk, unconsented biometric-data exploitation, and cross-linking of vehicle data with personal devices); and (4) a comparative survey of ten data-privacy laws/frameworks across nine jurisdictions (EU GDPR, EU ePrivacy Directive, US/California CCPA, Canada PIPEDA and CPPA, Singapore PDPA, China DSL and PIPL, Australia Privacy Act 1988, Qatar PDPPL), concluding that none of them contains CAV-specific provisions.
threat_model: The paper describes (rather than formally models) a set of qualitative threat scenarios drawn from the literature and general reasoning, concentrated in Section V.D ('Vehicle Hacking Leading to Unauthorized Access'): attackers exploiting Wi-Fi/Bluetooth/cellular connectivity to gain unauthorized access to a vehicle's data and, in more severe cases, to remotely manipulate safety-critical functions such as braking, steering, acceleration, or to disable airbags/anti-lock brakes. It also references, at a high level and without technical detail or its own experiments, AI-driven threats: data-poisoning attacks against onboard AI training data leading to unsafe driving decisions, deepfake-based identity attacks against biometric authentication enabling unauthorized vehicle access, and adversarial-ML manipulation of perception models (e.g., road-sign misclassification) and of models trained on driving-behavior data to extract private user information. These are presented as risk narratives with citations to prior work, not as attacks the authors implement, simulate, or evaluate. Additional non-hacking privacy threats discussed include third-party/insurer/marketer misuse of shared driving, location, and biometric data, and stalking/harassment enabled by unauthorized access to location trails.
limitations: The review is non-systematic: no stated search methodology, database coverage, time window, or inclusion/exclusion criteria, so completeness and selection bias cannot be assessed. Claims throughout are largely qualitative and assertive ('can,' 'may,' 'could') rather than evidenced by the authors' own data, incident statistics, or case studies; cited risk scenarios (e.g., stalking, kidnapping via travel-pattern leakage, deepfake biometric spoofing) are illustrative rather than empirically demonstrated within this paper. The widely repeated '300 TB/year per CAV' statistic is sourced from a non-peer-reviewed industry blog (ref. [11], Tuxera) rather than a primary measurement study, and is used without corroboration. The technical AI-security material (data poisoning, adversarial ML, deepfakes) is treated in a single paragraph with no technical depth, no threat formalization, and no evaluation, so it functions as a brief pointer to related concerns rather than a contribution. The regulatory comparison (Table III) is a static, point-in-time summary of named laws without depth on enforcement track records or CAV-specific case law.
